retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Molecular Level -- SRY
retrieved_docs.content: The SRY gene becomes transactivated by the product of the WT1, which binds to the SRY promoter region. [40] SRY binds to a testis-specific enhancer of SOX9 core (TESCO) in mice along with steroidogenic factor 1 (SF1). [12] SF1 and SRY work to upregulate levels of SOX9. When the production of SRY reduces SOX9, and SF1 maintains its expression.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoalphalipoproteinemia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A meta-analysis of four prospective American studies (Framingham Heart Study, Lipid Research Clinics Prevalence Mortality Follow-up Study, Coronary Primary Prevention Trial, Multiple Risk Factor Intervention Trial) was published by Gordon et al. in 1989. An increase of 1 mg/dL of HDL cholesterol was associated with a significant coronary heart disease risk decrease of 2% in men and 3% in women. [38] A study published in April 2022 by Dong et al. showed that many gene loci identified with low HDL levels are associated with various types of cancer. [45] Genes with the highest prevalence of damaging variants were: ABCA1 (Chronic myelogenous leukemia) [46] Stab1 (Bladder cancer and Acute Myelogenous Leukemia) [47] OSBPL1A CPS1 (Hepatocellular carcinoma) [48] CD36 (Ovarian cancer) [49] LRP1 [50] ABCA8 (Hepatocellular carcinoma) [51] GOT2 AMPD3 (Gastrointestinal stromal tumors and lung cancers) [52] [53] WWOX IRS1 (Medulloblastoma, breast cancer, and osteosarcoma) [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Sebaceous Carcinoma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sebaceous carcinoma originates from sebaceous glands and can manifest in any location where these glands are present. The transcription enhancer binding factor LEF1 mutation plays a critical role in skin tumor development, characterized by complete silence in sebaceous carcinomas. At the same time, sebaceomas and sebaceous adenomas may also present mutations related to this factor.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma  -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cirrhosis is a significant step in viral carcinogenesis for hepatocellular carcinoma. Integration of the hepatitis B virus genome into the host genome is the primary pathogenesis for oncogenesis in HBV. Insertion of viral genome in telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT ) promoter sites of the human genome resulting in mutation accounting for 60% of HCC cases. [4] Other genetic alterations include mutations in TP53 (affecting cell cycle), beta-1 catenin (CTNNBI), axis inhibitor-1 (AXINI), AT-rich interaction domain-containing protein 1A (ARID1A), and ARID2(chromatin proliferation).

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Hematopoiesis -- Structure -- Regulation
retrieved_docs.content: T-cell acute leukemia-1 (TAL-1) which is necessary to keep multipotent stem cells multipotent and quiescent and is an essential regulator of hematopoiesis.  TAL-1 complexes with a variety of other transcription factors (E47/E2A, LMO2, GATA1–3, Ldb1/2, Ldb1, ETO, Runx1, ERG, FLI1) to determine differentiation into myeloid or lymphoid cells. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Etiology -- Genetics
retrieved_docs.content: Over 100 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and other genes have been linked to an increased risk of prostate cancer, including hereditary prostate cancer gene 1, various androgen and Vitamin D receptor genes, HPC1, HPC2, HPCX, CAPB, mutL homolog 1 (MLH1), mutS homologs 2 and 6 (MSH2 and MSH6), postmeiotic segregation increased 2 (PMS2), homeobox B13 (HOXB13), checkpoint kinase 2 (CHEK2), nibrin (NBN), BRCA1-interacting protein C-terminal helicase 1 (BRIP1), ataxia telangiectasia mutated (ATM), and the TMPRSS2-ETS gene family, such as TMPRSS2-ERG and TMPRSS2-ETV1/4 , all of which tend to promote cancer cell growth. [26] [34] [36] (Note: This is only a partial listing. Clinically significant germline mutations are discussed in subsequent sections.)

retrieved_docs.title: Sideroblastic Anemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: SIDBA1 has been reported to be caused by various mutations, including missense, nonsense mutations, and mutations at promoter or enhancer regions. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Meige Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Focal dystonias also exhibit a genetic component in their etiopathogenesis. At the cellular level, mutations in the TOR1A gene appear to cause a disturbed exchange of vesicles into and out of the nucleus, which in turn causes transcriptional dysregulation. [38] [39] A similar mechanism is implicated in other primary focal dystonias, such as mutations in central nervous system transcriptional factors such as TAF1 and THAP1 . [40] Studies involving animal and human models show evidence of specific genetic mutations that may lead to the disrupted development of the neuronal network.

retrieved_docs.content: Genes for pro-a1 and pro-a2 chains are transcribed

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Telomere And Telomerase -- Molecular Level -- Telomerase
retrieved_docs.content: The synthesis of a telomere involves a reverse transcriptase telomerase, which functions as an RNA-dependent DNA polymerase. Telomerase is present in germline and stem cells and has enhanced activity in cancer cells. This enzyme is responsible for elongating telomeres by de novo addition of TTAGGG sequences onto 3' chromosome ends to prevent replicative cellular senescence. [10] Telomerase is a ribonucleoprotein structure comprising a functional RNA component and a catalytic reverse transcriptase component. The RNA component houses a template for the synthesis of telomeric DNA. The functional RNA component in humans is called hTERC or hTR [11] . It is encoded by the TERC gene located at the 3q26 region of the chromosome. The reverse transcriptase component is called hTERT and is encoded by the TERT gene located at chromosome 5p13.33. [12] While the telomerase core complex mainly consists of the 2 main components, hTERC and hTERT, essential supportive proteins exist to properly function the entire telomerase structure. Tcab1, Gar1, Nhp2, Reptin, and Pontin are proteins required for telomerase assembly and the proper recruitment of chromosomes. [13] [14] Next, the proteins responsible for stabilizing the telomerase structure are TEP1 and dyskerin. Lastly, the additional protein subunits, Es1p, and Es3p, aid in the assembly and maturation of the catalytic complex. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry of Platelet Activating Factor -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: PAFR transcript-1 is mostly involved in inflammation and pathological processes and is synthesized in response to stimulation by PAF in a positive feedback manner through the activation of NF-kB. PAFR transcript-2 is controlled by various hormones and cytokines such as estrogen, TGF-beta, retinoic acid, or T3. Retinoic acid and thyroid hormone are potent inducers of PAFR transcript-2. Estrogen also increases PAFR transcript-2 levels, and TFG-beta decreases its levels. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Development -- Development and Sex Determination of Gonads
retrieved_docs.content: The SRY gene encodes for a transcription factor that activates the testis-specific enhancer (TESCO) of a related autosomal gene known as SOX9 (SRY-Box Transcription Factor 9). The SOX9 gene is essential for the initiation of Sertoli cell differentiation from supporting cell precursors. These supporting cells would otherwise differentiate into follicle cells (specifically granuloma cells). [11] Besides SRY, there are other factors such as steroidogenic factor 1 (which is encoded by the gene NR5A1), which contribute to Sertoli cell differentiation. [12] SRY expresses exclusively in the supporting cells in the genital ridges, which differentiate into Sertoli cells. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Gastrulation -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: The differentiation of pluripotent stem cells to lineage-specified cells within the endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm is marked by the down-regulation of pluripotency markers (Oct 4, Nanog, Sox 2, etc.) in conjunction with the activation of lineage-specific gene expression, including microRNAs. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) have been demonstrated to be enriched in germ layers, specifically targeting TGFB to promote mesoderm and restrict or block neuroectoderm. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcifying Epithelioma of Malherbe -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Further research revealed that proliferating cells in human pilomatrixomas exhibit strong staining with antibodies targeting LEF-1, a marker for hair matrix cells. Additionally, S100 proteins have been identified as biochemical markers useful for characterizing pilomatrixomas. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Urethral Folds -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Development of external genitals occurs by three main pathways: Androgen-dependent Androgen-independent Endocrine/environmental factors

retrieved_docs.title: Duane Retraction Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: DRS is commonly a sporadic disorder, but in 10% of cases, it may be familial. Both autosomal dominant and recessive forms of DRS have been documented. In cases of isolated DRS, the DURS1 gene on chromosome 2q31 has been identified. [7] The autosomal dominant pattern of DRS is associated with mutations in the CHN1 gene, which affects the guidance of the growing abducent nerve axons to the lateral rectus. [8] [9] In DRS with associated malformation syndromes, the spalt-like transcription factor 4 (SALL4) gene on chromosome 20 has been implicated. [10] These syndromes include Okihiro syndrome, Duane-radial ray syndrome, and acro-renal-ocular syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2554
retrieved_docs.content: Bard-Pic syndrome and primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas are both mapped to cancer of pancreas, indicating that Bard-Pic syndrome is related to pancreatic cancer and that the primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas represents a specific form of pancreatic cancer. This connection highlights Bard-Pic syndrome as a risk factor for pancreatic malignancy and underscores the clinical relevance of identifying tumors located in the pancreatic head.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome has a mapped association with the development of cancer of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection is important for clinicians, as patients diagnosed with Bard‑Pic syndrome may require heightened surveillance for pancreatic malignancy and tailored management strategies to address the potential increased risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 216
retrieved_docs.content: where the primary site is not determined. Overall, the knowledge base integrates metastatic and secondary lesion concepts with primary cancer sites, clarifies the distinction between benign and malignant tumor cells, maps hierarchical relationships among organ‑specific metastases (especially thymic and endocrine gland involvement), and connects procedural interventions such as nephrectomy to their associated renal malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2581
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant and tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are both classified as abnormal cells of disease and are linked to cancer; other and unspecified primary, with a specific association to pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

Ocular diagnostic procedures are organized under uncategorized histopathology procedures, a parent category that also includes anatomy, indicating that eye histopathology forms part of a broader anatomical and pathological assessment framework.

retrieved_docs.content: carcinomas under the broader category of primary malignant neoplasms. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured view of digestive system cancers, especially pancreatic malignancies, their metastatic characteristics, and their placement within a comprehensive neoplasm classification system that spans multiple organ sites and includes both well‑defined and uncertain neoplastic entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional network that connects genetic syndromes, primary gastrointestinal cancers, various metastatic malignancies, and skin melanomas, providing essential context for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is associated with an increased risk of pancreatic cancer, linking this genetic disorder to malignancies of the pancreas. Within the Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Digestive System, pancreatic cancer is grouped with other gastrointestinal tumors such as colon cancer (سرطان القولون) and malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث); these entities occupy child positions under broader parent categories that include pancreatic cancer, malignant lip cancer, and neoplasms of uncertain behavior. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is explicitly mapped to pancreatic cancer, reinforcing its placement in this digestive‑system hierarchy. Metastatic disease is organized under the concept of secondary malignancies (النقيلة). Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان) and metastatic malignant neoplasms to lymph nodes of the neck and head‑and‑neck region are classified as secondary malignancies and as non‑hematologic malignant

This interconnected framework aids in understanding the spread patterns of malignancies, their organ‑specific manifestations, and their placement within the larger taxonomy of cancer diagnoses.
